title: Tunnel Vision vs Scattered Brain
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: When you're either locked-in on one thing or can't focus on anything.

sections.heading: What Tunnel Vision and Scattered Attention Look Like

content.text: Under threat, your attention narrows to a laser: tunnel vision locks you onto the threat and filters out everything else. This is survival mode—great for fleeing danger, terrible for driving home or having a conversation. When the threat passes but arousal stays high, your attention scatters: you can't focus on anything, thoughts jump around, and tasks feel impossible to start or finish. Both patterns are attentional dysregulation caused by a nervous system stuck in the wrong gear.
What this looks like in frontline work

items: Tunnel vision: Hyper-focused on one task, missing everything else happening around you.
Can't shift attention: Stuck on one thought or problem, unable to move on.
Scattered brain: Can't hold focus, jumping between tasks without finishing any.
Missing obvious details: 'How did I not see that?'
Difficulty tracking conversations: People talk, you hear noise.
Reading the same sentence five times without absorbing it.

lines: Two sides of the same issue:
Tunnel vision = attention locked under threat. Scattered brain = attention dysregulated after threat. Both mean your nervous system hasn't reset to baseline.

sections.heading: Why Attention Gets Stuck or Scattered

content.text: Your attentional system is controlled by arousal. High arousal (threat mode) narrows attention to immediate threats—tunnel vision. When arousal drops but doesn't reset to baseline, your prefrontal cortex (executive control) stays offline and attention scatters. You're too activated to focus but not activated enough to lock on. Sleep debt, stimulants (caffeine, nicotine), and unprocessed stress keep arousal stuck in the middle zone where attention fails.

items: Chronic hypervigilance: Always scanning for threats = no attentional flexibility.
Sleep debt: Executive control breaks down first when tired.
Stimulant overuse: Caffeine and nicotine spike arousal but don't improve focus under stress.
Screen time overload: Constant novelty trains your brain to seek distraction.
Unprocessed critical incidents: Intrusive thoughts hijack attention.

lines: Why this works: Reducing baseline arousal restores prefrontal control over attention. Single-tasking and minimizing distractions rebuild attentional endurance. Sleep and arousal regulation restore flexible focus.

content.text: You can't force focus when your nervous system is dysregulated. Start by lowering arousal, then rebuild attention endurance with single-tasking and short focus intervals.

items: Name the mode: 'I'm in tunnel vision' or 'My brain is scattered.' Awareness interrupts the loop.
Reset arousal first: 60-second breathwork or cold water on face before trying to refocus.
Single-task for 10 minutes: Set a timer. Do one thing. Stop when timer ends. Repeat.
Remove distractions: Phone away, notifications off, one browser tab, one task.
Use external structure: Timers, checklists, and alarms replace willpower.
Improve sleep for three days: Even small sleep gains restore executive control.

lines: Field rule: Attention follows arousal. Lower arousal first, then rebuild focus endurance with short intervals.

content.text: When your partner is in tunnel vision, they genuinely don't hear you. When they're scattered, they're not ignoring you—they can't hold focus. Both patterns frustrate everyone, but criticism makes it worse.

items: Name the pattern without judgment: 'You seem locked in right now. Want to take a break?'
Reduce competing demands: One task, one conversation at a time.
Use written reminders: Text or notes instead of verbal-only communication.
Support lowering arousal: Suggest a walk, breathing, or a short break before important conversations.
'You're not listening': They literally can't process multiple inputs when dysregulated.
Multitasking demands: 'Can you do this while you finish that?' guarantees failure.
Criticism: 'Why can't you just focus?' Shame doesn't fix attention.

content.text: Attentional dysregulation that persists despite rest and reduced stress may indicate ADHD, TBI, or other conditions. Get assessed if focus problems are chronic and impairing function.

items: Attention problems were present before frontline work (possible ADHD).
History of head injuries or concussions.
Focus deficits persist despite improved sleep and reduced stress.
Work or home function is deteriorating due to inability to focus.
Safety is compromised (driving, work tasks, parenting).

content.text: Use the worksheet to track attentional patterns and practice single-task focus intervals. Goal: rebuild attention endurance one short interval at a time.

lines: 1) Log one moment of tunnel vision or scattered brain.
3) Single-task for 10 minutes (one thing, timer set).
4) Log whether you completed the interval. Repeat daily.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Tunnel Vision vs Scattered Brain
worksheets.intro: Use this to track attentional dysregulation and practice single-task focus intervals.

question_groups.label: When did tunnel vision or scattered brain show up this week?
question_groups.hint: Examples: locked onto one task, couldn't shift attention, jumped between tasks, couldn't finish anything.

question_groups.label: What was your arousal level when attention failed?
question_groups.hint: High arousal = tunnel vision. Mid-range arousal = scattered. Both mean nervous system isn't at baseline.

question_groups.label: What two focus-rebuild actions will you practice this week?
question_groups.hint: Examples: 10-minute single-task intervals, arousal reset before refocus, remove distractions.

numbered_prompts.hint: What did your body do first?
What exact behavior will you use next time?

worksheets.closing_note: Attention follows arousal. Reset nervous system first, then rebuild focus endurance.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Tunnel Vision vs Scattered Brain - Partner/Team Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this with a partner to reduce frustration around attentional dysregulation.

question_groups.label: What does tunnel vision or scattered brain look like in me?
question_groups.hint: Observable signs: not responding when spoken to, jumping between tasks, unable to finish anything, hyper-focused and unaware of surroundings.

question_groups.label: What helps when my attention is dysregulated?
question_groups.hint: Examples: gentle interrupt, suggest a break, lower demands, give written reminders.

question_groups.label: What's one agreement we'll try this week?
question_groups.hint: Example: 'I'll name the pattern when I notice it. You'll help me reset arousal before refocusing.'

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset?
What specific repair happens after a rupture?

worksheets.closing_note: Keep check-ins short, specific, and repeatable.
